replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering updated: that ain't it, Rob - they're rack-based. The mechanism I remember was just with beveled gears. No worries, thanks!

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
If a circle has 1/n the radius of another circle it will revolve around it n+1 times. That's because there is one revolution involved in doing so no matter what size the 2nd circle is. 
You can demonstrate it with something like a coffee mug and a coin, revolve one around the other, then the other way round. More convincingly a dinner plate around a pen top.

author: @offgridmanpolktn
text:
Hey the old man is back.
   Math was a major for high school for me, and have always tinkered with mechanical toys. But I know my basic understanding of gears pulley's and associated interaction and that of any of my classmates knew these things to pass basic college entrance exams.
  So knew the outcome of your project when seeing the piece s.
   My high school was in the seventies but my sister's didn't start until the 80's when what was called new math was introduced. Neither has been much good at managing money since, even with 4 year college degree s.
   Thank God our Department of Edu is getting cleaned out and since the teachers union nationwide spent something like 80 percent of their disposable income on funds trying to get Biden and Harris into office. So good riddance.
Sorry for politics.
   Mainly so disappointed on the downhill trend on all levels of education.
  It's great that you explain these to people, but some of it seems like a common sense knowledge. Hopefully the people you teach will pass on.
X'cus the rambling.
✌️😎

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - to me maths is a language

author: @Allwestconstruction
text: Hiya mate, speaking of gear ratio, i was thinking of making a car charger that you can take with you with no fuel, a small ramp that you drive your car tire onto, then have the car weight pushing down on ramp that somehow would have gears that would turn the generator with gear ratios to have it run for an hour

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text: You would have to have a massive gear ratio that would turn a 1" down movement to many turns of the generator

replies.author: @spehropefhany
replies.text: Maybe consider a bladder driving an air motor. Say you got 30% overall efficiency, 350kg on one tire, 6” of motion (0.15m), the energy would be about 16J, not a heck of a lot. A USB power bank you can put in your pocket holds way more energy even if the overall efficiency was much higher than my estimate- even if it was an impossible 100%. It’s always good to do back of envelope physics calculations to see if it makes any sense before worrying about details. A 20,000 mAh 3.6V power bank is 74Wh or 266,000J, with maybe 60% efficiency, so about 160,000J vs. 50J theoretical maximum from the tire.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
You could work out what energy was stored mate - it's just weight and distance that will give you energy in joules - J=mgh
Joules equals mass times gravity time height

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering here is something i would like to do numbers on- I work with large excavators, i can pickup a 2 ton rock and place it on a 5' high wall in about 15 seconds, the fuel I would guess be not much maybe 1/8th of a gallon

replies.text: @spehropefhany I thinking of something very simple, kinda like a spare tire for an EV to charge in a pinch

author: @LoganKearsley
text: The important "hidden" thing in both cases is just that at least one of the gears is allowed to orbit--i.e., the axis can move, in addition to or in place of the gears spinning about its axis. Both mechanisms are effectively planetary gear systems, just warped into unusual shapes.
